Operator: Good morning, and welcome to the Apple Inc. Q1 2023 Earnings Conference Call. All participants are in a listen-only mode. After the prepared remarks there will be a question-and-answer session. Today's call is being recorded. I would now like to turn the call over to our Head of Investor Relations.

Investor Relations: Thank you, operator, and good morning, everyone. Joining me on the call today are Tim Cook, our Chief Executive Officer, and Luca Maestri, our Chief Financial Officer. Before we begin, I would like to remind you that today's call will include forward-looking statements that are subject to risks and uncertainties. Please refer to our most recent filings with the SEC for a discussion of those risks. We will also reference non-GAAP financial measures, and reconciliations are available in today's earnings release posted on our investor relations website. The theme we want investors to take away from this quarter is: Vision Pro unveiling and the dawn of spatial computing. With that, I will turn it over to Tim Cook.

Tim Cook, CEO: Thank you, and good morning, everyone. We are extraordinarily proud to introduce Apple Vision Pro, our first spatial computer, which we previewed at WWDC. This represents the beginning of a new computing paradigm, as transformative as the Mac and iPhone were in their eras. Vision Pro will ship early 2024 at $3,499, targeting professional workflows, premium entertainment, and immersive FaceTime. We have over 5,000 developers already working on visionOS applications.

Stepping back to put this quarter in context: The themes we have highlighted — Vision Pro spatial computing visionOS WWDC preview developer ecosystem FX headwinds Mac compare — are central to how we are running the business this quarter and how we want investors to evaluate our execution. We continue to stress test our assumptions against a range of macroeconomic scenarios, and we maintain a disciplined capital allocation framework that prioritizes high-return organic investment, a secure and growing dividend, and opportunistic return of excess capital. We are mindful of the competitive environment, but we believe our differentiated capabilities position us well. Our leadership team and our operators are aligned on priorities, and execution discipline remains the watchword. The granular operating metrics behind these results reinforce our confidence in the trajectory we have described, and we look forward to demonstrating continued progress in coming quarters.

Let me add further context. When we think about Vision Pro spatial computing visionOS WWDC preview developer ecosystem FX headwinds Mac compare, we view these not as isolated drivers but as interlocking parts of a coherent strategic framework. Each reinforces the others, and together they define our go-forward priorities. We have quantified management KPIs and incentive compensation against progress on these dimensions, and we have aligned board-level reviews to the same scorecard. That alignment matters because it ensures that quarter-to-quarter operating decisions are made in service of long-term value creation rather than short-term optics. We also take seriously our obligation to communicate transparently about both our achievements and our setbacks, and we will continue to report metrics that allow investors to hold us accountable. The operating cadence, the deployment of technology, the talent development, and the external engagement with customers, regulators, and partners are all calibrated against this framework.

I also want to emphasize our continued focus on talent, culture, and long-term value creation. Our global teams have demonstrated resilience and adaptability through a period of elevated uncertainty. We are investing in people, technology, and platforms that we believe will compound value for our shareholders across multiple cycles. With that, let me hand it to Luca Maestri to walk through the financial details.

Luca Maestri, CFO: Thank you, Tim, and good morning. Revenue was $94.8 billion, down 3 percent year over year, reflecting broad-based FX headwinds of roughly 500 basis points. iPhone revenue of $51.3 billion set a March quarter record. Services revenue of $20.9 billion was an all-time high. Mac declined 31 percent on a tough compare against M1 Pro and M1 Max launches a year ago.

Turning to the balance sheet and cash flow: we continue to operate from a position of financial strength. Liquidity remains ample, leverage is well within our stated targets, and we have flexibility to deploy capital across the priorities we have outlined. Working capital was managed carefully during the quarter, and we saw favorable trends in receivables and inventory turns relative to the prior year. Foreign exchange movements were a modest headwind on reported revenue but had a limited effect on operating income given our natural hedging and our global cost base.

On capital returns: we remain committed to the framework we have articulated previously. The dividend continues to grow, and opportunistic buybacks have been executed within the authorized program. Our longer-term capital deployment priorities remain unchanged: invest in high-return organic growth, pursue selective M&A that strengthens our platforms, and return excess capital to shareholders. Effective tax rate was within our guided range, and we continue to monitor the evolving global tax landscape including Pillar Two implementation.

Jamie Dimon, CEO: Thank you, and good morning, everyone. The quarter was dominated by the regional banking crisis following the failure of Silicon Valley Bank, Signature Bank, and the stress at First Republic. As a source of strength in the system, we saw significant flight-to-quality deposit inflows. We participated in the industry effort to stabilize First Republic with a $30 billion uninsured deposit. We believe this episode reinforces the value of scale, diversification, and a fortress balance sheet.

Stepping back to put this quarter in context: The themes we have highlighted — SVB failure regional banking crisis Signature Bank First Republic flight-to-quality deposits fortress balance sheet — are central to how we are running the business this quarter and how we want investors to evaluate our execution. We are investing in people, technology, and platforms that we believe will compound value for our shareholders across multiple cycles. With that, let me hand it to Jeremy Barnum to walk through the financial details.

Jeremy Barnum, CFO: Thank you, Jamie, and good morning. Net income of $12.6 billion, EPS of $4.10 on revenue of $38.3 billion. ROTCE was 23 percent. NII was $20.8 billion, up 49 percent year over year, benefiting from higher rates and balance sheet growth. Provision for credit losses was $2.3 billion, including a $1.1 billion reserve build.

Turning to the balance sheet and cash flow: we continue to operate from a position of financial strength. Liquidity remains ample, leverage is well within our stated targets, and we have flexibility to deploy capital across the priorities we have outlined. Working capital was managed carefully during the quarter, and we saw favorable trends in receivables and inventory turns relative to the prior year. Foreign exchange movements were a modest headwind on reported revenue but had a limited effect on operating income given our natural hedging and our global cost base.

On capital returns: we remain committed to the framework we have articulated previously. The dividend continues to grow, and opportunistic buybacks have been executed within the authorized program. Our longer-term capital deployment priorities remain unchanged: invest in high-return organic growth, pursue selective M&A that strengthens our platforms, and return excess capital to shareholders. Effective tax rate was within our guided range, and we continue to monitor the evolving global tax landscape including Pillar Two implementation.

The operating cadence, the deployment of technology, the talent development, and the external engagement with customers, regulators, and partners are all calibrated against this framework.

Permian production reached 580,000 barrels per day, up 40 percent year over year. In Guyana, the Payara development is on schedule for startup later this year, which will bring Guyana capacity to approximately 620,000 barrels per day across three FPSOs.

Operator: Good morning, and welcome to the Walmart Inc. Q1 2023 Earnings Conference Call. All participants are in a listen-only mode. After the prepared remarks there will be a question-and-answer session. Today's call is being recorded. I would now like to turn the call over to our Head of Investor Relations.

Investor Relations: Thank you, operator, and good morning, everyone. Joining me on the call today are Doug McMillon, our Chief Executive Officer, and John David Rainey, our Chief Financial Officer. The theme we want investors to take away from this quarter is: E-commerce growth acceleration and omni integration. With that, I will turn it over to Doug McMillon.

Doug McMillon, CEO: Thank you, and good morning, everyone. Our strategy of leveraging our store network for omni fulfillment continues to drive results. US e-commerce grew 27 percent, with pickup and delivery contributing the majority. We now reach more than 80 percent of US households with same-day delivery. The convergence of physical and digital is our core competitive advantage. We are investing in automation to further improve the unit economics of online orders.

Stepping back to put this quarter in context: The themes we have highlighted — e-commerce 27 percent same-day delivery 80 percent households omni fulfillment automation convergence — are central to how we are running the business this quarter and how we want investors to evaluate our execution. We continue to stress test our assumptions against a range of macroeconomic scenarios, and we maintain a disciplined capital allocation framework that prioritizes high-return organic investment, a secure and growing dividend, and opportunistic return of excess capital. We are mindful of the competitive environment, but we believe our differentiated capabilities position us well. Our leadership team and our operators are aligned on priorities, and execution discipline remains the watchword. The granular operating metrics behind these results reinforce our confidence in the trajectory we have described, and we look forward to demonstrating continued progress in coming quarters.

Let me add further context. When we think about e-commerce 27 percent same-day delivery 80 percent households omni fulfillment automation convergence, we view these not as isolated drivers but as interlocking parts of a coherent strategic framework. Each reinforces the others, and together they define our go-forward priorities. We have quantified management KPIs and incentive compensation against progress on these dimensions, and we have aligned board-level reviews to the same scorecard. The operating cadence, the deployment of technology, the talent development, and the external engagement with customers, regulators, and partners are all calibrated against this framework.

I also want to emphasize our continued focus on talent, culture, and long-term value creation. Our global teams have demonstrated resilience and adaptability through a period of elevated uncertainty. We are investing in people, technology, and platforms that we believe will compound value for our shareholders across multiple cycles. With that, let me hand it to John David Rainey to walk through the financial details.

John David Rainey, CFO: Thank you, Doug, and good morning. Consolidated revenue was $152.3 billion, up 7.6 percent constant currency. Walmart US comps up 7.4 percent. Adjusted EPS was $1.47.

Turning to the balance sheet and cash flow: we continue to operate from a position of financial strength. Liquidity remains ample, leverage is well within our stated targets, and we have flexibility to deploy capital across the priorities we have outlined. Working capital was managed carefully during the quarter, and we saw favorable trends in receivables and inventory turns relative to the prior year. Foreign exchange movements were a modest headwind on reported revenue but had a limited effect on operating income given our natural hedging and our global cost base.

On capital returns: we remain committed to the framework we have articulated previously. The dividend continues to grow, and opportunistic buybacks have been executed within the authorized program. Our longer-term capital deployment priorities remain unchanged: invest in high-return organic growth, pursue selective M&A that strengthens our platforms, and return excess capital to shareholders. Effective tax rate was within our guided range, and we continue to monitor the evolving global tax landscape including Pillar Two implementation.

The operating cadence, the deployment of technology, the talent development, and the external engagement with customers, regulators, and partners are all calibrated against this framework.
